chunk_summary: Guidelines for interpreting CBC, ferritin, and B12/folate labs in adolescents, emphasizing resource-adapted decision rules for anemia and micronutrient deficiencies.
learning_objectives: Understand the role of ferritin as an iron status indicator and its limitations in inflammatory states.
Learn decision rules for diagnosing and managing microcytic and macrocytic anemia in adolescents.
Identify appropriate therapeutic approaches for B12 and folate deficiencies, including oral and parenteral regimens.
Apply pragmatic strategies for lab interpretation in resource-limited settings.
key_facts: Ferritin is an acute-phase reactant; low levels confirm iron deficiency, but normal/high levels may not exclude it in inflammation or chronic disease.
Empiric iron trial: A hemoglobin rise of 1–2 g/dL in 2–4 weeks confirms iron-responsive anemia.
Thalassemia trait: Suspect with marked microcytosis, high RBC count, and family history; confirm with hemoglobin electrophoresis.

For B12 deficiency: Oral high-dose therapy (1000 mcg daily) is acceptable for stable adolescents; IM therapy (250–1000 mcg alternate days for 1–2 weeks) is preferred for severe cases or neurologic symptoms.
Do not use folate-only therapy when neurologic symptoms are present; treat B12 deficiency first.
reference_quote: Ferritin concentrations are recommended for assessing iron status but should be interpreted alongside inflammation markers like CRP when infection or chronic disease is present. (WHO eLENA)
document_key_topics: Vitamin B12 and folate deficiency treatment
